lessons.title: std::flat_map and std::flat_set: Array-Backed Associative Containers
lessons.motivation: std::map and std::set are C++ workhorses, but under the hood they use a red-black tree: every node is a separate heap allocation, and traversing to the next element means chasing a pointer. On read-heavy workloads — lookups and iteration — this causes constant cache misses. C++23 introduced std::flat_map and std::flat_set: containers with the same interface but storing data in sorted vectors. Binary search over a vector is far more cache-friendly than a tree, and for the typical use case of "fill once, query many times" this delivers a measurable speedup without changing calling code.
lessons.theory:
std::flat_map<K, V> stores keys and values in two separate sorted vectors. Lookup is performed by binary search over the keys vector in O(log n), just like std::map, but accessing consecutive keys walks contiguous memory — the CPU cache is used efficiently. Header: <flat_map>. std::flat_set<T> similarly stores elements in a single sorted vector; header: <flat_set>.

The interface of flat_map and flat_set deliberately mirrors map/set: operator[], at(), find(), contains(), insert(), erase(), and range-for over key-value pairs. Switching from map to flat_map often amounts to changing one type name. One important difference concerns iterators: dereferencing a flat_map iterator returns a proxy pair of references (std::pair<const K&, V&>), which is a temporary object — structured bindings must be declared as auto [k, v], not auto& [k, v].

Performance depends on the access pattern. flat_map wins for reads, iteration, and small-to-medium datasets (thousands to tens of thousands of elements). It loses when elements are inserted frequently in the middle: each insertion requires shifting elements in O(n). If the input data is already sorted, the std::sorted_unique tag lets you construct the container without an extra sort pass. Decision rule: build the table once and only read it afterward — prefer flat_map; insert and erase frequently at arbitrary positions — use regular map.

flat_map exposes its underlying storage through keys() and values(), which return const references to the internal vectors. This allows passing the key list to an algorithm without copying, enables SIMD-friendly sequential access, or simply retrieves the size without iterating. The default backing container is std::vector, but any random-access container (e.g. std::deque) can be substituted via template parameters.

examples.title: Basic flat_map: same interface as map
examples.expectedOutput:
Alice: 98
Bob: 87
Carol: 92
contains Dave: false
examples.explanation: flat_map behaves exactly like map: the duplicate assignment scores["Alice"] = 98 updates the value, iteration visits elements in sorted key order, and contains() uses binary search. Notice auto [name, score] without &: a flat_map iterator returns a proxy pair of temporary references, and binding by lvalue reference does not compile.

examples.title: flat_set and the sorted_unique tag
examples.explanation: flat_set keeps elements in a sorted vector and silently drops duplicates on insert. The std::sorted_unique tag signals to the flat_map constructor that the input is already ordered and unique — the container skips the O(n log n) sort and copies the data directly. Only use this tag when you can guarantee order and uniqueness: violating either is undefined behavior.

examples.title: Configuration table: find() and keys()
examples.expectedOutput:
port = 8080
retries = 3
missing not found
key count: 5
examples.explanation: flat_map is an excellent fit for configuration tables: built once at startup, then used exclusively for reads. find() performs binary search and returns an iterator or end(). keys() gives direct access to the internal keys vector without copying — handy for passing to algorithms or logging. Similarly, values() exposes the values vector.

lessons.title: std::move_only_function and std::spanstream
lessons.motivation: std::function<Sig> is a convenient wrapper for storing any callable, but it has a hidden constraint: the stored callable must be copyable. This blocks capturing a unique_ptr, std::promise, or any other move-only resource in a lambda you want to store in std::function. C++23 solves this with std::move_only_function<Sig>, which lifts the copyability requirement and accepts any movable callable. The second utility in this lesson is std::spanstream: std::stringstream allocates a buffer on the heap at construction, which is unacceptable in embedded code that forbids allocations or in real-time handlers. std::ospanstream and std::ispanstream operate over a fixed external buffer with zero heap activity.
lessons.theory:
std::move_only_function<Sig> is declared in the <functional> header and behaves like std::function, but is a move-only type: it can be moved but not copied. This makes it possible to store lambdas that capture std::unique_ptr, std::promise, file descriptors, or any other resource for which copying is not defined. The call syntax is unchanged: f(args...). Two methods specific to std::function — .target() and .target_type() — are absent in move_only_function, which slightly simplifies the implementation and yields marginally better performance.

Practical use cases include task queues (each task exclusively owns a resource), callback chains (promise/future), and processing pipelines where each stage holds unique ownership over its data. Attempting to copy a move_only_function is a compile error — an explicit, statically checked contract rather than silent undefined behavior or a runtime exception.

std::spanstream is a family of stream types from the <spanstream> header that operate over external memory. std::ospanstream takes a std::span<char> and writes data into that buffer via operator<<, allocating no heap memory. After writing, the .span() method returns a span bounded to the bytes actually written. std::ispanstream takes a std::span<const char> and parses it with operator>> exactly like std::istringstream would. Both classes inherit from std::basic_spanstream, which supports combined read-write access when needed.

The key value of spanstream is predictable memory usage: the buffer lives exactly as long as the caller decides, with no hidden heap traffic. This matters in real-time systems, embedded platforms with restricted heaps, or high-throughput servers where the allocator becomes a point of contention. One limitation: ospanstream cannot grow — if more data than the buffer size is written, the output is silently truncated and the stream enters an error state, which must be checked via os.good() or os.tellp().

examples.title: move_only_function: storing a lambda that captures unique_ptr
examples.explanation: The lambda captures a unique_ptr via std::move, making the lambda itself move-only. std::function would refuse to store such a callable because copying it is impossible. std::move_only_function accepts the move without complaint: each task in the vector holds exclusive ownership of its resource.

examples.title: move_only_function as a parameter and transferring ownership
examples.explanation: The run() parameter accepts move_only_function by value, transferring ownership of the callable along with all its captured resources. The second block shows an explicit move: after std::move(f), f is in a valid-but-unspecified state and g fully owns the stored function. Trying to copy f instead of moving it would be a compile error.

examples.title: ospanstream and ispanstream: streaming I/O without heap allocation
examples.explanation: ospanstream writes the text representation of values directly into the stack array buf. After writing, os.span() returns a span bounded to the bytes actually written — not the entire buffer. ispanstream parses the buffer exactly as istringstream would parse a string, but without any allocation. Note the explicit std::span<const char>: the ispanstream constructor takes a span, not a pointer, and there is no implicit conversion from a braced-init list.
